title: Divine Manifestations | Bhagavad Gita Chapter 10 Verses 36- 42 | HG Amitasana Das | HKM Mumbai
speaker: HG Amitasana Dasa

segments.text: So today we will be discussing from the 10th chapter of the Gita, so the title is Divine
Manifestations, text number 36 to 42. So this chapter has 42 verses based on the teachings
of our beloved spiritual master, His Divine Grace A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Srila Prabhupada.
We will chant this verse together. Please repeat after me.
I am also the gambling of cheats, and of the splendid I am the splendor. I am victory,
I am the adventure, and I am the strength of the strong. So this is in reply to Arjuna's
question, can you please reveal some of your glories, some of your glorious manifestations,
some of your potencies.
Your energies. So in reply to that Krishna says that my potencies, the creations are
infinite, but he is trying to list out few of those creations just to give us a clue
of his glorious manifestation or his greatness. So I am also the gambling of cheats. It is
said that
in this world we see at times we can cheat others, we can break the laws, we can break
the rules and go scot free. But when it comes to the Lord, we cannot cheat Him. When it
comes to His laws, they are very stringent. We cannot break them and go scot free because
nobody noticed us.
happens that we break the laws and we can maneuver out of it without getting noticed.
Sometimes we, you know, various laws, could be income tax, we can do something whereby
the income is shown differently or whatever it is and may not get noticed.
When it comes to the stringent laws of material nature, we cannot escape.
The laws of karma, every action has a reaction, good or bad.
So here it says, if we try to cheat the Lord, we will get cheated.
We cannot deceive the Lord.
And at times Lord also, we have many examples in the Mahabharata, where Lord actually cheated
someone.
Now we will also discuss what those instances were and for what purpose it was done.
We have the example of Krishna taking the side of the Pandavas because Duryodhana was
asked what do you want, you want me or you want my army?
So Duryodhana being very shrewd, what is the use of Krishna when he is not going to fight?
Better let me choose the army.
So he was very clear about his choice.
He chose the army.
But Arjuna, was very, very happy to have Krishna with him.
And Krishna in the Gita boldly declares, my devotee shall never perish.
He will always protect his devotees, even at the cost of, mind you, we can underline
the statement.
of he breaking his own promise. Even at the cost of he becoming infamous. There are many
many instances where one thing is he can be upright, he can show that he will not break
any rules, but for his devotees sake, for the devotees protection, he may even break
the rules. That's why he is also called Bhaktavatsala.
So it so happened that when the Mahabharata war was on, after ten days, the fighting was
happening very fiercely and there was no sign of you know the warfare getting to a conclusive
end. Both sides were you know fighting fiercely.
And on one side there was
Fierce, you know, people like Bhishma, Dronacharya, the other side we had Krishna, Arjuna and
the Pandavas and their army.
And it was very clear that so long as these great warriors like Dronacharya, Bhishma are
there fighting on behalf of Kauravas, they are not going to easily get overpowered and
let the Pandavas win.
So after the tenth day, Krishna went to Yudhishthira Maharaj, Duryodhana, sorry Dronacharya, he
was the military teacher, in fact he taught the military art to the Pandavas also.
And it was very difficult to defeat him.
And unless he gets defeated.
You can't defeat the army of the Kauravas.
So Krishna on the tenth day, knowing that somehow Dronacharya has to be killed, he went
to Yudhishthira Maharaj.
He had a weakness that if he hears about the death of his son, Ashwatthama, he will get
completely so to say shaken, shattered, rattled.
And then in that state he can be killed, not otherwise.
So Yudhishthira Maharaj was called Dharmaraj.
He would never, never, never break any principle.
He was very upright, man of high integrity, would never tell a lie at any cost.
So Krishna knowing that if Dronacharya has to be killed, he has to be somehow told that
Ashwatthama is dead.
So he asked Yudhishthira Maharaj, go and tell Dronacharya that Ashwatthama is dead.
Now how can Yudhishthira Maharaj tell a lie?
He was reluctant.
He said, I can't say the lie.
But then through a trick, he made a statement to Dronacharya, which means Ashwatthama hata
kunjara.
Hata means dead.
Dronacharya.
Kunjara means an elephant.
So Ashwatthama, elephant by name Ashwatthama is killed.
But this last two words of the statement, hata, kunjara, he very softly mentioned.
And blew the conch.
When Dronacharya heard this statement from the mouth of Yudhishthira Maharaj, who is
Dharmaraj.
Who is known for his uprightness.
He would never ever tell a lie.
He had no doubt.
Sometimes you know you want to verify the fact.
You want to double check.
Since he had come from the mouth of Yudhishthira Maharaj, he got completely convinced that
this has actually happened.
And he was, the death of his son, he was completely shaken, completely shattered.
And then he sat down.
And at that time he was killed.
Now morally speaking, you may say, no.
No.
Morally speaking, you may say this is wrong, this is unfair.

There is a context behind all these things.

ultimate goal was to enthrone the Pandavas who were righteous.
Righteous kings.
And establish dharma.

If she would glance him, the entire body becomes like a stone.
